name: Restore the accepted PTX hot-band anchor after the failed live-state trim

summary.name: Restore the accepted PTX hot-band anchor after the failed live-state trim
summary.hypothesis: The round-3 PTX live-state trim changed the wrong part of the recovered winner: despite a 2-register drop, active warps stayed flat and runtime exploded. Restoring the exact accepted PTX surface should recover the 24.293 ms anchor and re-establish a clean base for the next exploit.
summary.expected_bottleneck: Known register-limited plateau on the accepted 128x128 PTX surface; this direction is a recovery step, not a new bottleneck attack.

score_breakdown.reason: Fastest path back to the accepted baseline after an unambiguous PASS_LOSS on the active PTX family.

ranking_notes: The previous step already proved this exploit is not ready for iteration on top of the current code.
Restore first so the next diagnosis is anchored on a real winner rather than on a known loss surface.
Do not interpret this as a new optimization idea; it is recovery to the accepted baseline.
summary.stop_condition: Stop if the restored surface does not recover to within noise of the accepted base runtime.

target_hotspots.explanation: Launch Statistics is carrying metric Registers Per Thread.
Occupancy is carrying metric Achieved Occupancy.

expected_local_changes: Restore the implementation surface from measured commit f768e80d950fa4cd036ea003b32af972278df540.
Discard the round-3 scope-narrowing edit rather than layering another exploit on top of a loss surface.
Keep the current workflow/workload support files on HEAD while only restoring compiled implementation code.

guardrail_metrics.reason: Tensor activity is part of the active bottleneck picture and should not drop after the next code edit.
Latency-hiding is already weak; active warps should not regress.
long scoreboard stalls are consuming 6.87% of active warp issue slots.
barrier stalls are consuming 5.95% of active warp issue slots.

notes: Node C may now implement this one candidate.
